food_subgroup: Poultry-based rice bowl with sauce and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composed smoked duck rice bowl with black pepper sauce and sous vide egg. FOODON_00002473 (duck food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked duck meat as the primary protein component. No composite bowl code exists in FoodEx2 for this dish; closest_parent applied to the dominant protein.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955005 covers duck as a food substance. No specific smoked duck bowl concept exists; closest_parent applied.

allergens: gluten (soy sauce in black pepper sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 120 g smoked duck slices, 180 g butter rice, 50 g black pepper sauce, and one sous vide egg (~50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Roasted duck (NDB 05140), Cooked white rice with butter (SR Legacy), Whole egg sous vide (NDB 01123), Black pepper sauce (estimated from condiment data)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Pepper Smoked Duck Bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average component analysis: smoked/roasted duck (USDA NDB 05140, ~32% by weight), butter rice (USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice + butter, ~47% by weight), sous vide egg (USDA NDB 01123, ~13% by weight), and black pepper sauce (~8% by weight estimated from generic brown sauce data). Smoking process assumed to concentrate protein and fat relative to raw duck. Values represent a plausible central estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based meals — analogous estimate based on white rice GI (Atkinson et al., 2008, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pepper Smoked Duck Bowl. The dominant glycemic contributor is butter rice (white rice cooked with butter). White rice GI is reported at approximately 64 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., 2008, AJCN 87:258S-268S). The addition of fat (butter), protein (duck, egg), and sauce moderates the overall glycemic response. A composite GI of approximately 56 is estimated, consistent with published data on mixed protein-fat-rice meals reducing GI by 10–15% relative to plain white rice. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 77.9) / 100 ≈ 43.6, rounded to 43.8. Serving carbohydrate: 380 g × 0.205 = 77.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1596 mg per serving) from black pepper sauce, butter, and smoked duck curing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce portions, as a single serving may provide approximately 70–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~56) driven by butter rice; moderate carbohydrate load (~78 g per serving yields GL ~44
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the glycemic load per serving is high (~44); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and choosing a smaller rice portion can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from duck skin and butter (~2.9 g per 100 g; ~11 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that this dish contributes meaningful saturated fat and sodium per serving; requesting skinless duck and reduced butter rice can lower the saturated fat and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Smoked Duck Bowl is a Western-Asian fusion rice bowl dish served in Singapore cafes and casual dining establishments. It consists of thinly sliced smoked duck breast, typically cured and cold-smoked to develop a rich, savoury, and slightly smoky flavour with a tender, moist texture. The duck is served over butter rice — steamed white rice finished with butter for a fragrant, slightly rich base — and accompanied by a black pepper sauce that is bold, peppery, and mildly sweet. A sous vide egg, cooked at low temperature to achieve a custard-like, jammy yolk and silky white, is placed on top, adding creaminess and additional protein. The dish is hearty and satisfying, combining the umami depth of smoked poultry with the comfort of buttered rice and the aromatic heat of black pepper. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-to-high calorie meal (~740 kcal per serving) with meaningful protein (~41 g per serving) from duck and egg, moderate fat (~31 g per serving) including saturated fat from duck and butter, and a significant carbohydrate load from the rice (~78 g per serving). Sodium content is elevated due to the smoked duck curing process and the black pepper sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 56 (medium), moderated by the fat and protein content relative to plain white rice. Clinically, portion control and sauce reduction are advisable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or dairy.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Pepper Smoked Duck Bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for individual components (roasted/smoked duck, butter rice, sous vide egg, black pepper sauce). GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the moderating effect of fat and protein. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data; no direct dish-level measurement was available in HPB Singapore Foodo or other primary regional sources as of the access date. 2. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore cafe bowl portions; actual restaurant serving may vary. 3. Sodium content may be higher in restaurant preparations depending on sauce recipe and duck curing method. 4. The dish is listed under 'Coffee' cuisine in the source GrabFood data, which likely reflects the cafe category tag rather than the dish's culinary origin; cuisine has been corrected to 'Western-Asian Fusion' for accuracy. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.4), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (185.0), calcium_mg (22.0), iron_mg (1.2), cholesterol_mg (95.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.18), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.2), vitamin_b6_mg (0.22), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.35), folate_mcg_dfe (14.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.6), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (145.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (1.4), selenium_mcg (12.0), and water_g (58.5) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually confirmed by a primary source for this composite dish; these values should be treated as indicative. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g as all fields have been estimated from component data. 6. Halal status is listed as false because smoked duck from non-certified sources may not be halal; verification with the specific restaurant is required. 7. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are applied at closest_parent level as no composite dish code exists for this preparation.
